body:
DIRECTIVE: TWO PROBLEMS. (A) the terminal death -- instrument it, do not guess. (B) the overfit/low-value results -- these are a SUBSTRATE problem, not a search problem, and the fix is structural. Good work on pass 9: holding the additive-only line against the delete instruction was correct, and retracting the false stuck-validation lead without shipping code was exactly right.

=========================================================================
PART A -- THE TERMINAL DEATH: MEASURE IT, DO NOT THEORISE
=========================================================================
Two deaths, no clean shutdown, no crash event. SLS37 at ~15s, SLS38 at ~23min at the r3->r4 boundary. Free memory 1.84GB/8.1GB. The deeper-each-time pattern fits a MEMORY CLIMB, not a logic bug. Windows Update/VSS is correlated but unproven. Stop hypothesising -- instrument it so the next run PROVES the cause.

A1 MEMORY TELEMETRY (do this first, it is cheap). Log free/used memory at every phase boundary AND at every regime boundary inside Phase 2/3B, via TerminalInfoInteger(TERMINAL_MEMORY_AVAILABLE) and MQLInfoInteger(MQL_MEMORY_USED). One line each, house style. If memory climbs monotonically across regimes and the death lands where it crosses a threshold, we have the answer in one run.
A2 ARRAY-GROWTH AUDIT. The r3->r4 boundary is where it died. Audit every ArrayResize / dynamic array in the per-regime calibration path for growth that is never released between regimes. A per-regime buffer that is resized-up and never freed will climb exactly like this. Report any array whose size is a function of regime index or accumulates across the regime loop.
A3 CHECKPOINT + RESUME. Calibration must survive a terminal death. Persist per-regime results (IC, weights, sample counts, verdicts) to disk AS EACH REGIME COMPLETES, under the existing guarded-marker scheme. On restart, if a valid partial checkpoint exists, RESUME from the next unfinished regime rather than restarting from zero. This alone turns a fatal 23-minute loss into a 30-second recovery, and it is worth more than the root cause.
A4 REDUCE PRESSURE. While calibrating: no chart redraws, no unnecessary object creation, release large temp arrays as soon as a regime completes. Report peak memory per phase.
Deliver the memory-per-regime table as the headline. If it climbs, we have it. If it is flat, the cause is external and we look elsewhere -- but we will KNOW.

=========================================================================
PART B -- THE OVERFIT IS A SUBSTRATE PROBLEM (the real fix)
=========================================================================
THE HONEST DIAGNOSIS. P1 baseline 29.8% WR. r1/r2 rejected with holdout gaps of -27.1pp and -21.0pp. We keep calling the rejection a success -- and the PROTECTION is working. But a system that refuses EVERY time is not succeeding; it is telling us the signal is not there IN THE FORM WE ARE ASKING FOR. Stop celebrating the refusal and fix what is being refused.
ROOT CAUSE, MEASURED: hard 9-regime classification scores IC = +0.001, p = 0.92 out-of-sample. IT PREDICTS NOTHING. Phase 3B is therefore searching 562,500 weight combinations WITHIN BUCKETS THAT CARRY NO INFORMATION. You cannot fit a good model on a meaningless partition. The overfit is not a search failure -- it is a substrate failure. Fix the substrate and the search stops hallucinating.

B1 OPTIMISE ON THE SOFT STATE, NOT THE HARD BUCKET. Phase 3B must fit weights against the CONTINUOUS membership vector (soft, probability-weighted), not the argmax integer regime. Every training sample contributes to every regime in proportion to its membership. This (i) triples effective sample size per regime -- directly attacking the thin-cell overfit, (ii) removes the hard-border information destruction, and (iii) is measured to recover signal (IC +0.001 -> +0.036, p=0.92 -> 0.013). PREDICT in the warrant: holdout gap should shrink materially from -27/-21pp.
B2 STOP OPTIMISING WIN-RATE. Optimise CALIBRATED EXPECTANCY. A 29.8% baseline WR is being fed into a weight search whose objective is essentially hit-rate. Our own measurement says the real edge lives at 42-48% WR with winners 1.6x losers and forward moves of +0.6 to +1.0 ATR. The objective function is therefore fighting the actual edge. Change the Phase 3B objective to REPLICATED, NET-OF-COST EXPECTANCY (mean R per trade), with win-rate reported but never optimised. This single change may explain most of the low-value results.
B3 GATE BEFORE YOU FIT. Do not fit weights on all bars. Fit only on bars the system can actually READ -- narrow ensemble cone, low membership entropy, meaningful expected move. MEASURED: gated bars give +1.008 ATR (t=+4.11, p<0.0001) versus +0.343 ungated. Fitting on ungated noise is what generates the fake edge that then collapses. Fit where the signal is; refuse elsewhere.
B4 SHRINK THE SEARCH, NOT JUST THE RESULT. 562,500 combinations against one split guarantees the winner is the luckiest noise draw (expected best-of-K noise ~ +0.188R -- which IS the -19..-44pp collapse). Replace the brute grid with (i) far fewer candidates, generated from the measured IC vector rather than exhaustively enumerated, and (ii) a selection penalty scaled to log(candidates tested). A search that cannot be fooled is better than a search that is caught afterwards.
B5 KILL THE LOW-VALUE BRANCHES HONESTLY. Any component/branch whose measured contribution is indistinguishable from zero after shrinkage must not receive weight, must not add conviction, and must be reported as inert. Do not carry passengers. If 3 of 6 components are inert on this instrument, say so and let the other 3 breathe.

=========================================================================
DISCIPLINE
=========================================================================
Every change a Change Warrant with a numeric prediction, scored after. Additive and reversible -- keep holding that line, it was right. Zero-Regression: nothing working may degrade. Learned on TRAIN only. Report per-regime memory, the new holdout gaps, and the gated-vs-ungated expectancy table. Do not resume doctrine work until (A) the terminal survives a full calibration and (B) at least one regime passes fit-check on the soft substrate.

If B1-B3 are right, the next calibration should show holdout gaps in single digits and at least one regime keeping its learned weights. If they still collapse, that is a real finding and I want to see it -- report it, do not force a pass.
